verses.text: Then spake Elisha unto the woman, whose son he had restored to life, saying, Arise, and go thou and thine household, and sojourn wheresoever thou canst sojourn: for the LORD hath called for a famine; and it shall also come upon the land seven years. And the woman arose, and did after the saying of the man of God: and she went with her household, and sojourned in the land of the Philistines seven years. Guidance: The Shunammite warned. The chapter opens with the Shunammite woman of chapter 4 — the woman whose son had been raised from the dead — receiving another prophetic word from Elisha. 'The LORD hath called for a famine; and it shall also come upon the land seven years.' Hebrew qara YHWH la-ra'av — 'the LORD has summoned the famine.' (The Hebrew construction implies that the LORD is the agent — He has called for the famine to come; the famine is His instrument of discipline.) Seven years is the standard biblical 'long famine' duration (the same length as Joseph's famine in Gen 41:30, and as the famine Elijah implicitly produced in 1 Kgs 17-18 — though that one ended after about 3.5 years; cf. Luke 4:25). Elisha's instruction: 'Arise, and go thou and thine household, and sojourn wheresoever thou canst sojourn.' She is to relocate to a place where she can endure the famine. She is given prophetic foresight unavailable to the rest of Israel. She obeys without question: 'The woman arose, and did after the saying of the man of God.' She takes her entire household and 'sojourned in the land of the Philistines seven years.' Philistine territory along the coast — west of Israel, in what is now southern Israel/Gaza Strip — apparently retained better food production during the famine (likely Phoenician trade-routes and coastal-plain agriculture sustained them). For seven years she lives as a sojourner (Hebrew gur — temporary resident, the same word for the patriarchs' status in Canaan) among the Philistines. The chapter is showing us the LORD's care for those who have served His prophet. The Shunammite had built Elisha a room (4:8-10); she had taken his prophecy seriously even when it offered her a hope she had given up (4:16); she had refused to accept her son's death (4:19-37). Now Elisha repays her with private prophetic information about national disaster. She gets seven-year warning. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's care often arrives as prophetic foresight given to specific individuals. He warns His own; they act on the warning; they survive what others suffer. The Shunammite is not given the warning because she is more deserving than other Israelites; she is given it because she has been positioned in faithful relationship with the LORD's prophet. The chapter is also setting up a kind of dramatic distance from the previous chapter's events. Chapters 6-7 ended with the Aramean siege lifted; this chapter opens with the Shunammite's seven-year sojourn either preceding or paralleling the siege-famine of chapter 6 (chronology in this section is loose). The narrator is using the Shunammite's story as a frame that will set up the chapter's larger moves.

And it came to pass at the seven years' end, that the woman returned out of the land of the Philistines: and she went forth to cry unto the king for her house and for her land. And it fell on a day, that the king talked with Gehazi the servant of the man of God, saying, Tell me, I pray thee, all the great things that Elisha hath done. And it came to pass, as he was telling the king how he had restored a dead body to life, that, behold, the woman, whose son he had restored to life, cried to the king for her house and for her land. And Gehazi said, My lord, O king, this is the woman, and this is her son, whom Elisha restored to life. And when the king asked the woman, she told him. So the king appointed unto her a certain officer, saying, Restore all that was hers, and all the fruits of the field since the day that she left the land, even until now. Guidance: The Shunammite's return and the providential timing. After seven years she returns to Israel. Her property had presumably been confiscated or occupied during her absence; she goes to the king to petition for restoration. By extraordinary providence, the day she arrives at the palace, the king is in conversation with Gehazi — Elisha's former servant — asking him to recount Elisha's miracles. 'Tell me, I pray thee, all the great things that Elisha hath done.' (Note: this is presumably the same Gehazi who in 5:27 became a leper. The king's willingness to converse with him — and Gehazi's continued presence in royal circles — suggests either that the chapter's events occur before Gehazi's leprosy, OR that leprosy did not absolutely exclude him from royal audience, OR that the chapter sequence is not strictly chronological. The narrator does not resolve the question.) Just as Gehazi is recounting the Shunammite's resurrection-miracle, the Shunammite herself walks in with her petition. Gehazi recognizes her: 'My lord, O king, this is the woman, and this is her son, whom Elisha restored to life.' The timing could not be more remarkable. The king has been hearing about Elisha's greatest miracles; the very woman whose son embodies one of those miracles steps into the room mid-sentence. The king verifies the story with the woman herself; she confirms. The king's response is generous: 'Restore all that was hers, and all the fruits of the field since the day that she left the land, even until now.' Not just her property; not just back-rent; all the produce her land would have generated during the seven absent years. Total restoration plus accumulated yield. The chapter is teaching us several principles. (1) The LORD's providential timing is itself a kind of miracle. The Shunammite did not orchestrate her arrival; the conversation she walked into was not arranged by Gehazi or by the king. The LORD positioned the testimony of her past miracle to coincide with her petition for present help. The providence is invisible but precise. (2) Past miracles continue to bear interest. The resurrection that happened years ago in chapter 4 is now repaying the Shunammite with property restoration in chapter 8. The LORD's care does not have an expiration date. (3) Gehazi — despite his moral failure in chapter 5 — still serves as a witness to Elisha's ministry. The LORD can use even compromised servants as carriers of legitimate testimony. The chapter is also showing us a Jehoram (presumably) who is at this moment functioning correctly. He listens to prophet-related testimony; he restores property fairly; he honors the LORD's miracle by honoring its beneficiary. Even apostate kings have lucid moments.

And Elisha came to Damascus; and Benhadad the king of Syria was sick; and it was told him, saying, The man of God is come hither. And the king said unto Hazael, Take a present in thine hand, and go, meet the man of God, and enquire of the LORD by him, saying, Shall I recover of this disease? So Hazael went to meet him, and took a present with him, even of every good thing of Damascus, forty camels' burden, and came and stood before him, and said, Thy son Benhadad king of Syria hath sent me to thee, saying, Shall I recover of this disease? Guidance: Elisha visits Damascus. The narrative jumps to Aram. Elisha — the prophet whose intelligence about Aramean military movements had frustrated Ben-hadad's campaigns in chapter 6 — is now in Damascus, the Aramean capital itself. (The chapter does not explain why he is there; possibly he had been summoned because of the king's illness, or possibly he was fulfilling the third commission Elijah had received at Horeb in 1 Kgs 19:15 — 'anoint Hazael king over Syria.' The latter is the most likely reason; this is the visit that activates that long-deferred prophetic charge.) Ben-hadad is sick. News reaches him that 'the man of God is come hither' — Elisha. The king's response is striking: he sends his senior officer Hazael with a state-gift and a prognostic inquiry. 'Take a present in thine hand, and go, meet the man of God, and enquire of the LORD by him, saying, Shall I recover of this disease?' Two remarkable details. (1) Ben-hadad addresses Elisha as 'the man of God' (ish ha-elohim) — the proper Hebrew title for a true prophet. The Aramean king uses correct theological terminology. (2) Ben-hadad sends 'enquire of the LORD by him' — Hebrew dirsh et-YHWH me'oto — 'inquire of the LORD through him.' The Aramean king explicitly invokes the LORD's name as the divine to consult. Whether Ben-hadad has converted (echo of Naaman in chapter 5?) or simply pragmatically recognizes the LORD as the most reliable oracle-giver, he treats the LORD as the source he wants to consult. The contrast with Ahaziah in chapter 1 — who had sent to Baal-zebub of Ekron for the same kind of prognostic inquiry — is sharp. The pagan king now consults the LORD; the apostate Israelite king consulted Baal-zebub. Hazael complies: he takes '40 camels' burden' of gifts — an enormous quantity, every good thing Damascus produces (silks, metals, foods, treasures) loaded on 40 pack-animals. He arrives at Elisha's presence and addresses him: 'Thy son Benhadad king of Syria hath sent me to thee, saying, Shall I recover of this disease?' 'Thy son' (ben'kha) is a deferential address — Ben-hadad positions himself as junior to Elisha. The chapter is showing us the prophetic ministry's growing international reputation. The same prophet who serves the LORD's covenant people is now consulted by the king of the enemy power. The LORD's prophet is becoming an internationally-recognized authority. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's authority does not respect national borders. His prophet is His prophet whether he is in Samaria, Dothan, or Damascus.

And Elisha said unto him, Go, say unto him, Thou mayest certainly recover: howbeit the LORD hath shewed me that he shall surely die. And he settled his countenance stedfastly, until he was ashamed: and the man of God wept. And Hazael said, Why weepeth my lord? And he answered, Because I know the evil that thou wilt do unto the children of Israel: their strong holds wilt thou set on fire, and their young men wilt thou slay with the sword, and wilt thou dash their children, and rip up their women with child. Guidance: The dual oracle and Elisha's tears. Elisha's response to Hazael's inquiry is famously enigmatic: 'Go, say unto him, Thou mayest certainly recover: howbeit the LORD hath shewed me that he shall surely die.' Hebrew lekh emor-lo chayoh tichyeh ve-hir'ani YHWH ki-mot yamut — 'go say to him, you may certainly live; but the LORD has shown me that he will certainly die.' Two truths. (1) The disease itself is not fatal; Ben-hadad could recover from it. (2) Nonetheless, he will die — by another means. Elisha is signaling without yet stating that Hazael himself will be the cause of the king's death. The Hebrew is deliberately layered. The chapter then stages a peculiar interaction. 'He settled his countenance stedfastly, until he was ashamed: and the man of God wept.' Hebrew vayyaamed et-panav vayyasem ad-bosh va-yevkh ish ha-elohim — 'he set his face and held it until he was ashamed, and the man of God wept.' Elisha stares fixedly at Hazael — long, penetrating, until Hazael becomes uncomfortable. Then the prophet weeps. The strange detail is meaningful: Elisha is reading Hazael's coming character before Hazael has lived it. Hazael asks: 'Why weepeth my lord?' Elisha's answer is the chapter's most painful moment: 'Because I know the evil that thou wilt do unto the children of Israel.' Four atrocities Hazael will commit: (1) burn their strongholds; (2) slay their young men with the sword; (3) dash their children to pieces; (4) rip open pregnant women. Standard ancient Near Eastern war-atrocities — the four common patterns of total-war terror. (Amos 1:3-4 will later identify these specifically as Hazael's signature: 'they have threshed Gilead with threshing instruments of iron.') Elisha is reading the future of Hazael's reign — the atrocities he will commit during years of war against Israel — and weeping over them. The chapter is showing us the prophet's heart. He does not enjoy what he sees coming; he weeps. The information has been given him; the future is real; he grieves the suffering of his people that he cannot prevent. The chapter is teaching us that prophetic foresight is often a burden, not a privilege. Knowing what is coming is sometimes worse than being surprised by it. Elisha must look at the man who will burn his country's cities and tell him so to his face. The chapter is also setting up Hazael's transformation. The man standing before Elisha — currently a senior officer, deferential, well-spoken — will become the monstrous king of years to come. And Elisha said unto him, Go, say unto him, Thou mayest certainly recover: howbeit the LORD hath shewed me that he shall surely die. And he settled his countenance stedfastly, until he was ashamed: and the man of God wept. And Hazael said, Why weepeth my lord? And he answered, Because I know the evil that thou wilt do unto the children of Israel: their strong holds wilt thou set on fire, and their young men wilt thou slay with the sword, and wilt thou dash their children, and rip up their women with child. Guidance: The dual oracle and Elisha's tears. Elisha's response to Hazael's inquiry is famously enigmatic: 'Go, say unto him, Thou mayest certainly recover: howbeit the LORD hath shewed me that he shall surely die.' Hebrew lekh emor-lo chayoh tichyeh ve-hir'ani YHWH ki-mot yamut — 'go say to him, you may certainly live; but the LORD has shown me that he will certainly die.' Two truths. (1) The disease itself is not fatal; Ben-hadad could recover from it. (2) Nonetheless, he will die — by another means. Elisha is signaling without yet stating that Hazael himself will be the cause of the king's death. The Hebrew is deliberately layered. The chapter then stages a peculiar interaction. 'He settled his countenance stedfastly, until he was ashamed: and the man of God wept.' Hebrew vayyaamed et-panav vayyasem ad-bosh va-yevkh ish ha-elohim — 'he set his face and held it until he was ashamed, and the man of God wept.' Elisha stares fixedly at Hazael — long, penetrating, until Hazael becomes uncomfortable. Then the prophet weeps. The strange detail is meaningful: Elisha is reading Hazael's coming character before Hazael has lived it. Hazael asks: 'Why weepeth my lord?' Elisha's answer is the chapter's most painful moment: 'Because I know the evil that thou wilt do unto the children of Israel.' Four atrocities Hazael will commit: (1) burn their strongholds; (2) slay their young men with the sword; (3) dash their children to pieces; (4) rip open pregnant women. Standard ancient Near Eastern war-atrocities — the four common patterns of total-war terror. (Amos 1:3-4 will later identify these specifically as Hazael's signature: 'they have threshed Gilead with threshing instruments of iron.') Elisha is reading the future of Hazael's reign — the atrocities he will commit during years of war against Israel — and weeping over them. The chapter is showing us the prophet's heart. He does not enjoy what he sees coming; he weeps. The information has been given him; the future is real; he grieves the suffering of his people that he cannot prevent. The chapter is teaching us that prophetic foresight is often a burden, not a privilege. Knowing what is coming is sometimes worse than being surprised by it. And Hazael said, But what, is thy servant a dog, that he should do this great thing? And Elisha answered, The LORD hath shewed me that thou shalt be king over Syria. Guidance: 'Is thy servant a dog?' and the prophetic anointing. Hazael's response is one of the most striking moments of self-deception in the OT. 'But what, is thy servant a dog, that he should do this great thing?' Hebrew ki mah avdekha ha-kelev ki ya'aseh ha-davar ha-gadol ha-zeh — 'what is your servant, the dog, that he should do this great thing?' Hazael protests that he could never become the kind of monster Elisha is predicting. He calls himself 'the dog' (ha-kelev) — using a self-deprecating image — and finds the idea of committing such atrocities impossible. 'This great thing' (ha-davar ha-gadol ha-zeh) is heavy irony: the 'great thing' is mass murder; Hazael, perhaps with deliberate misdirection, perhaps with genuine inability to imagine himself doing it, recoils from the suggestion. Elisha's response cuts through: 'The LORD hath shewed me that thou shalt be king over Syria.' Hebrew her'ani YHWH otkha melekh al-aram — 'the LORD has shown me you as king over Aram.' Hazael will not just remain a senior officer; he will become king. And once he is king — once the constraints of being someone else's officer are gone — the atrocities Elisha has predicted will follow. The chapter is showing us a profound truth about human moral psychology. People in subordinate positions often genuinely cannot imagine themselves capable of the atrocities they would commit if elevated. Hazael as Ben-hadad's officer is shocked by the prediction. Hazael as king (within hours) will assassinate Ben-hadad and within years will commit exactly the atrocities he is now denying he could ever do. The chapter is teaching us a sobering principle: position changes character. Power reveals what was already there, even when the person at the lower position could not see it. Hazael's 'is thy servant a dog?' is the kind of question that prosperous and powerful people, looking back at the deeds they have done, often realize was asked too early in their career. They did not know yet what they were capable of. The chapter is also teaching us about the LORD's prophetic foresight reading the depths of a human soul before the soul itself has revealed them. The LORD knows what Hazael will become; Hazael does not. The prophet's foresight makes the chapter's tears appropriate.

So he departed from Elisha, and came to his master; who said to him, What said Elisha to thee? And he answered, He told me that thou shouldest surely recover. And it came to pass on the morrow, that he took a thick cloth, and dipped it in water, and spread it on his face, so that he died: and Hazael reigned in his stead. Guidance: The assassination. Hazael returns to Ben-hadad. The king asks what Elisha said. Hazael reports only the favorable half: 'He told me that thou shouldest surely recover.' He omits the second half of the prophecy ('the LORD has shown me that he shall surely die'). Ben-hadad receives the favorable report, presumably with relief. The next day, Hazael acts. 'He took a thick cloth, and dipped it in water, and spread it on his face, so that he died.' Hebrew vayyiqach ha-makhber vayyitbol ba-mayim vayyiphros al-panav vayyamot — 'he took the netting/cover, dipped it in water, spread it on his face, and he died.' A heavy wet cloth or quilted cover suffocates Ben-hadad in his sickbed. The method is undetectable — a sick man dying in his bed shows no obvious cause; observers would attribute it to the illness. But the narrator names the cause precisely: assassination by suffocation. 'And Hazael reigned in his stead.' Hazael ascends to the throne. The chapter is showing us how quickly a man's protests against the prediction of his own future evil dissolve when opportunity presents. Twenty-four hours earlier Hazael had cried 'is thy servant a dog?' Now he is suffocating his master in bed. The transition from 'I could never' to 'I just did' takes one day. The chapter is teaching us a brutal lesson about ambition. The same man who genuinely could not imagine harming the king becomes the king's killer once the path to the throne opens. The disease created the opportunity; Hazael did not invent the chance, but he seized it. The chapter is also teaching us that prophets sometimes have to deliver knowledge that gets used in ways they would not endorse. Elisha's words to Hazael — 'you shall be king' — may have been the very catalyst that triggered Hazael to act. Elisha did not authorize the assassination; he predicted Hazael's eventual reign. Hazael interpreted the prediction as a license to expedite the timeline. The chapter is showing us the responsibility of receivers of prophecy. Knowledge of one's future does not authorize one to bring it about by criminal means. Hazael had a choice; he chose murder; the prophecy is fulfilled but Hazael is also condemned by his own deed. The chapter is preparing the reader for Hazael's long career as Aram's most destructive king — a career that the rest of 2 Kings will trace as it inflicts the predicted atrocities on Israel.

And in the fifth year of Joram the son of Ahab king of Israel, Jehoshaphat being then king of Judah, Jehoram the son of Jehoshaphat king of Judah began to reign. Thirty and two years old was he when he began to reign; and he reigned eight years in Jerusalem. And he walked in the way of the kings of Israel, as did the house of Ahab: for the daughter of Ahab was his wife: and he did evil in the sight of the LORD. Yet the LORD would not destroy Judah for David his servant's sake, as he promised him to give him alway a light, and to his children. Guidance: Jehoram of Judah introduced. The narrative shifts south. Jehoram son of Jehoshaphat ascends in Judah in the 5th year of Joram of Israel — about 848 BC. He is 32; he reigns 8 years; the chronology is tight (he dies around 841 BC, the same year Jehu's coup ends the Omride dynasty). The evaluation is grim: 'He walked in the way of the kings of Israel, as did the house of Ahab.' He is a Davidic king who has abandoned the Davidic spiritual pattern to imitate the apostate Omrides. The cause: 'For the daughter of Ahab was his wife.' Athaliah — daughter of Ahab and Jezebel — had married into the Davidic line. (This is the alliance Jehoshaphat had arranged in 1 Kgs 22:44 — peace with the king of Israel; the alliance was sealed by the marriage of the heir apparent. The decision has now produced catastrophic theological consequences.) Athaliah brings Phoenician Baal-worship into the Davidic court; her husband adopts the Omride spiritual pattern. 'He did evil in the sight of the LORD.' The same verdict the chapter has applied to the northern kings. But then a remarkable qualification: 'Yet the LORD would not destroy Judah for David his servant's sake, as he promised him to give him alway a light, and to his children.' Hebrew velo-avah YHWH le-hashchit et-yehudah le-ma'an david avdo — 'the LORD was not willing to destroy Judah for the sake of David His servant.' The Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7) protects the southern kingdom even when its current king is wicked. The 'light' (nir) given to David — the Davidic lamp, the continuing royal line — would not be extinguished. The chapter is teaching us a foundational theological truth about covenant. The LORD's covenant with David is unconditional in its dynastic permanence. Individual Davidic kings may sin; the LORD may discipline them; but the dynastic line will not be destroyed. The 'lamp of David' (1 Kgs 11:36; 2 Kgs 8:19; 2 Chr 21:7) will continue until the messianic culmination. The chapter is also teaching us that bad marriages have generational consequences. Jehoshaphat's strategic decision to marry his heir to Ahab's daughter — made for political-peace reasons — has produced a wicked Davidic king. The decision will soon produce something far worse: Athaliah's eventual coup that will nearly destroy the Davidic line (2 Kgs 11). The political-peace dividend has been bought at a near-fatal spiritual cost.

In his days Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah, and made a king over themselves. So Joram went over to Zair, and all the chariots with him: and he rose by night, and smote the Edomites which compassed him about, and the captains of the chariots: and the people fled into their tents. Yet Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah unto this day. Then Libnah revolted at the same time. Guidance: Edom and Libnah revolt. Two Judean territories revolt during Jehoram's reign. (1) 'Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah.' Edom had been a Judean vassal since David's conquest (2 Sam 8:14); through the time of Jehoshaphat (1 Kgs 22:47) only a deputy ruled, not a king. Now Edom shakes off Judean control and elects its own king. Jehoram responds militarily: 'Joram [Jehoram of Judah] went over to Zair, and all the chariots with him.' Zair is an unidentified location, probably on the Edom-Judah border. The campaign goes badly. 'He rose by night, and smote the Edomites which compassed him about, and the captains of the chariots: and the people fled into their tents.' Jehoram is surrounded in a night-engagement; he manages a hard breakout, killing the encircling Edomites; but his own troops scatter to their tents — battlefield defeat. The narrator's verdict: 'Yet Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah unto this day.' Edom remains independent permanently. The vassalage David had won and Solomon had consolidated and Jehoshaphat had retained is now lost forever. (2) 'Then Libnah revolted at the same time.' Libnah — a town in the Judean Shephelah on the Philistine frontier — also breaks away. The narrator pairs the two revolts to show the rapid disintegration of Judean authority. The chapter is teaching us about the political consequences of apostasy. Jehoram has joined himself to the Omride pattern; the LORD is disciplining him through territorial loss. The same kingdom Jehoshaphat had built has shrunk significantly within one wicked reign. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's covenant promises to David do not exempt His chosen line from temporal discipline. The Davidic dynasty will continue (v19); but individual Davidic kings who sin will lose vassals, lose battles, lose the geographical fruits of their predecessors' faithfulness. The chapter is teaching us a sobering principle: apostate leadership produces shrinking dominion. The map under Jehoram is smaller than the map under Jehoshaphat. The cost is real and visible.

(1) 'Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah.' Edom had been a Judean vassal since David's conquest (2 Sam 8:14); through the time of Jehoshaphat (1 Kgs 22:47) only a deputy ruled, not a king. Now Edom shakes off Judean control and elects its own king. Jehoram responds militarily: 'Joram [Jehoram of Judah] went over to Zair, and all the chariots with him.' Zair is an unidentified location, probably on the Edom-Judah border. The campaign goes badly. 'He rose by night, and smote the Edomites which compassed him about, and the captains of the chariots: and the people fled into their tents.' Jehoram is surrounded in a night-engagement; he manages a hard breakout, killing the encircling Edomites; but his own troops scatter to their tents — battlefield defeat. The narrator's verdict: 'Yet Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah unto this day.' Edom remains independent permanently. The vassalage David had won and Solomon had consolidated and Jehoshaphat had retained is now lost forever. (2) 'Then Libnah revolted at the same time.' Libnah — a town in the Judean Shephelah on the Philistine frontier — also breaks away. The narrator pairs the two revolts to show the rapid disintegration of Judean authority. The chapter is teaching us about the political consequences of apostasy. Jehoram has joined himself to the Omride pattern; the LORD is disciplining him through territorial loss. The same kingdom Jehoshaphat had built has shrunk significantly within one wicked reign. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's covenant promises to David do not exempt His chosen line from temporal discipline. The Davidic dynasty will continue (v19); but individual Davidic kings who sin will lose vassals, lose battles, lose the geographical fruits of their predecessors' faithfulness. The chapter is teaching us a sobering principle: apostate leadership produces shrinking dominion. The map under Jehoram is smaller than the map under Jehoshaphat. The cost is real and visible.

And the rest of the acts of Joram, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And Joram slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David: and Ahaziah his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Jehoram's regnal closing. The standard formula. The narrator cross-references the lost southern annals; notes Jehoram's death and burial in the city of David; names his successor Ahaziah. (2 Chr 21 gives a fuller account, including the gruesome detail that Jehoram died of incurable intestinal disease — a divine judgment specifically prophesied by Elijah from across the kingdom-boundary. The narrator in 2 Kings 8 records only the dignified formula.) The chapter is teaching us that even the wicked Davidic kings receive standard royal burial honors. The covenant's protection of the dynastic line includes the formal recognitions of legitimate succession. Jehoram, despite his evil, is buried 'with his fathers in the city of David' — the standard place. The covenant honors the office even where it must judge the man.

In the twelfth year of Joram the son of Ahab king of Israel did Ahaziah the son of Jehoram king of Judah begin to reign. Two and twenty years old was Ahaziah when he began to reign; and he reigned one year in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Athaliah, the daughter of Omri king of Israel. And he walked in the way of the house of Ahab, and did evil in the sight of the LORD, as did the house of Ahab: for he was the son in law of the house of Ahab. Guidance: Ahaziah of Judah introduced. Ahaziah (not to be confused with the earlier Israelite king of the same name in chapter 1) ascends in Judah in the 12th year of Joram of Israel — about 841 BC. He is 22 and reigns only one year. (His brief reign will end with him being caught in Jehu's coup against the Omrides — chapter 9 will record his death.) His mother is Athaliah — here called 'the daughter of Omri king of Israel' (technically she was Omri's granddaughter through Ahab; Hebrew bat ('daughter') can also mean 'descendant'). His maternal lineage is Omride; he 'walked in the way of the house of Ahab, and did evil in the sight of the LORD, as did the house of Ahab.' The narrator emphasizes the cause: 'For he was the son in law of the house of Ahab.' Hebrew chatan beit-achav — son-in-law/related-by-marriage to Ahab's house. The dynastic intermarriage Jehoshaphat arranged has now produced two consecutive wicked Davidic kings. The southern kingdom is now spiritually indistinguishable from the northern. The chapter is teaching us about generational transmission of apostasy. Jehoshaphat made one marriage decision; the consequences span three generations (Jehoshaphat's grandson and his descendants). The chapter is also setting up the dramatic events of chapter 9. Ahaziah will join Joram of Israel in a campaign against Hazael of Aram at Ramoth-gilead; Joram will be wounded; the two kings will retreat to Jezreel; Jehu's coup will begin precisely when both kings are convalescing together; both will die that day. The chapter is positioning all the pieces for the bloody collapse of the Omride dynasty in chapter 9.

And he went with Joram the son of Ahab to the war against Hazael king of Syria in Ramothgilead; and the Syrians wounded Joram. And king Joram went back to be healed in Jezreel of the wounds which the Syrians had given him at Ramah, when he fought against Hazael king of Syria. And Ahaziah the son of Jehoram king of Judah went down to see Joram the son of Ahab in Jezreel, because he was sick. Guidance: The Ramoth-gilead campaign and the convalescence at Jezreel. Ahaziah of Judah joins Joram of Israel in a renewed campaign against Hazael of Aram at Ramoth-gilead — the same city where Ahab had died in 1 Kgs 22, the perpetual border-contested town. (Hazael, now Aramean king, has presumably reasserted control over the region.) The campaign goes badly for Joram of Israel: 'The Syrians wounded Joram.' He is hit in battle and must withdraw. He retreats to Jezreel — the Omride summer-palace city in the broad valley of the same name, north of Samaria — to recover. The chapter then notes Ahaziah's fateful decision: 'Ahaziah the son of Jehoram king of Judah went down to see Joram the son of Ahab in Jezreel, because he was sick.' The southern king travels north to visit his wounded uncle (or first cousin, depending on exact reckoning; Athaliah was Joram's sister, so Ahaziah was Joram's nephew). The visit is a kinship-courtesy. The chapter is showing us the LORD positioning the pieces for the coming judgment. Both kings — the Israelite king and his Judean nephew — are now in Jezreel together; both are in the same city as Jezebel the queen mother (probably still alive in Jezreel); both are away from their respective armies; both are exposed. The chapter is setting up the perfect conditions for Jehu's coup to take them all out in a single day. The chapter is teaching us about providential setup. The LORD's judgments are often staged through ordinary events — battlefield wounds, family visits, royal convalescence — that converge at exactly the moment of execution. Joram's wound was not engineered by Jehu; Ahaziah's visit was not engineered by Jehu. But the LORD's purposes converge in their convergence. The chapter ends with the stage perfectly set. Chapter 9 will open with Elisha sending one of the sons of the prophets to anoint Jehu, and the long-prophesied destruction of the Omride dynasty will begin.
